title: Mahshi felfel
subtitle: Egyptian rice-stuffed peppers in tomato sauce
excerpt: Green peppers cored and filled with spiced rice and beef, then simmered upright in a tomatoey broth until the rice is cooked through and the peppers are tender. The same rice and meat filling as mahshi cromb, in a different vessel. The pepper wall seasons the filling from the outside as it cooks.
sourceNotes: Mahshi felfel is part of the Egyptian stuffed vegetable tradition. The same filling (spiced rice and minced meat) is used for all mahshi: cabbage, courgette, aubergine, and grape leaves. Stuffed peppers appear across the Middle East, the Balkans, and the Mediterranean; the Egyptian version uses the same spice combination as all Egyptian mahshi (cumin, cinnamon, allspice).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Peppers can be stuffed and refrigerated 1 day ahead.

content.text: Fill the peppers only two-thirds full with the rice and meat mixture. The rice swells as it cooks and a fully-packed pepper will split. Replace the pepper tops after filling to keep the steam inside.

Stand the peppers upright in a pot that holds them snugly so they support each other and do not tip. Pour the tomato broth around them (not over the tops). Cook covered at a low simmer for 45 to 50 minutes. The rice needs this time to cook fully inside the pepper.

items.prepNote: medium-large, tops removed and reserved, deseeded

content.text: Mix the {{short-grain-rice}}, {{beef-mince}}, {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{olive-oil}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together until well combined. Fill each {{pepper-green}} two-thirds full with the rice mixture. Replace the pepper tops. Stand the peppers upright in a snug pot. Mix the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{lemon-juice}}, and 200 ml of water and pour around the peppers. The liquid should come halfway up the peppers. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 45 to 50 minutes. Check at 40 minutes — pierce a pepper with a skewer and taste the rice for doneness. Serve with the tomato broth spooned around and plain yoghurt on the side.

items.fix: Fill only to two-thirds and maintain a very gentle simmer. A rolling boil will split the peppers

items.cause: Insufficient steam or not enough liquid
items.fix: Check the liquid level at 30 minutes and add more water if the tomato broth is running low. The steam inside the pepper cooks the rice — without moisture it stays raw

items.cause: Filling needs assertive seasoning before going into the pepper
items.fix: Taste a tiny amount of the raw filling and add more salt, cumin, and cinnamon before stuffing. The pepper wall absorbs some of the seasoning during cooking

content.text: Mahshi felfel is part of the Egyptian tradition of filling vegetables with spiced rice and meat. The same technique applied to courgettes produces mahshi kousa, to cabbage leaves produces mahshi cromb, to grape leaves produces warak dawali. In Egypt, making mahshi is a semi-ceremonial domestic activity — preparing a large quantity of different stuffed vegetables together for a family meal. Outside Egypt, stuffed peppers in tomato sauce are found across the Mediterranean, but the Egyptian spice combination of cumin and cinnamon is distinctive.
